In Shanghai, I've never felt more visible.
I've lived the last month in a hotel where every wall has at least one mirror. I can see myself when I wake up, when I go to sleep, when I change clothes, when I shit.
There's also a scale, so for the first time since high school, I'm weighing myself almost every day.
Going out into the city, I feel more eyes on me than normal. People comment about my hair, my eyes, or how I look like I've "aged well" for a foreigner.
Standing at the mirror, I feel so disconnected from the body in front of me. I feel like I can never trust how old or young I look, how muscular or flabby I look, or how attractive or ugly I am.
I look at the 3D model of me performing pre-programmed animations with the same sense of disconnection as looking at my bare body in the mirror.

Maybe the model is a truer form of myself because at least he knows he's performing for an audience, whereas when I look at myself in the mirror and try to convince myself I'm happy with my body, I don't know who I'm performing for.
One thing that the model and I have in common, though, is movement.
When I'm pushing my body to climb, run, or lift weights, the connection to the appearance of my body becomes so much less important than the capabilities of my body.
Our bodies are meant for so much more than to be looked at, and I need to remind myself of that more often.
